title: France - National Report (Cycle 4, Session 43)
text:
United Nations A
/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
General Assembly
Distr.: General
7 March 2023
English
Original: French
Human Rights Council
Working Group on the Universal Periodic Review
Forty-third session
1–12 May 2023
National report submitted pursuant to Human Rights
Council resolutions 5/1 and 16/21
*
France
* The present document is being issued without formal editing.
GE.23-04198 (E) 080124 090124

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
I. Introduction and process for the preparation of the report
1. Human rights are rooted in the philosophy of the Enlightenment and in the Declaration
of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen of 26 August 1789, and respect for those rights is
central to the values and legal system of France. France is heavily involved in protecting and
promoting all human rights at the national, regional and international levels.
2. France supported the establishment of the universal periodic review mechanism and,
as an elected member of the Human Rights Council, played an active part in the Council’s
foundation. During the third cycle of the universal periodic review, France accepted 239 of
the 297 recommendations, or 80 per cent. Since then, it has strived to implement the accepted
recommendations. Drawn up as part of the fourth cycle of the country’s universal periodic
review, the present report covers developments in the human rights situation in France and
progress made since the previous cycle.
3. The Ministry for Europe and Foreign Affairs coordinated the preparation of the
present report with other relevant ministries,1 in consultation with the independent
administrative authorities responsible for human rights protection and with the National
Consultative Commission on Human Rights, the national human rights institution. The
Government has adapted the report to take into account the Commission’s comments.
II. Improvement of the normative and institutional frameworks
for the protection of human rights
A. International instruments2
4. Since the last recommendations were issued under the universal periodic review:
• France ratified Protocol No. 16 to the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights
and Fundamental Freedoms (European Convention on Human Rights), which came
into force on 1 August 2018, enabling the Constitutional Council, the Council of State
and the Court of Cassation to refer disputes to the European Court of Human Rights
for an opinion.
• The parliament approved ratification of the International Labour Organization (ILO)
Violence and Harassment Convention, 2019 (No. 190) on 28 October 2021. The
instrument of ratification is awaiting deposit.
5. On other recommendations:
• France does not plan to withdraw its declaration on article 4 of the International
Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination. It interprets the
reference made therein to the principles of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
and to the rights set forth in article 5 of the Convention as releasing the States parties
from the obligation to enact anti-discrimination legislation which is incompatible with
the freedoms of opinion and expression and of peaceful assembly and association
guaranteed by those texts.
• France does not plan to withdraw its declaration on article 27 of the International
Covenant on Civil and Political Rights. In accordance with its Constitution, France
does not recognize the existence of the “ethnic, religious or linguistic minorities”
referred to in the Covenant.
• The provisions of the International Convention on the Protection of the Rights of All
Migrant Workers and Members of Their Families fall partially within the competence
of the European Union. States members of the European Union are not entitled to
accede to the Convention unilaterally. Nevertheless, the rights of migrant workers,
whatever their situation with regard to residency rights, are protected by French
domestic law, European Union law, the European Convention on Human Rights and
the other international human rights instruments to which France is a party.
• While the constitutional law of France prevents the State from ratifying the ILO
Indigenous and Tribal Peoples Convention, 1989 (No. 169), it adheres to several of
2 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
the principles established in the Convention and implements them in accordance with
its legal framework.
• The ILO Domestic Workers Convention, 2011 (No. 189) conflicts with the principle
of equal treatment contained in the Convention, rendering its ratification impossible
under current French law. In France, the system applicable to such workers is based
on agreements negotiated between the social partners, which ensures a balance
between employee protection and the needs of employers. Nevertheless, ratification
is under consideration.
6. In 2001, France extended a standing invitation to the special procedures of the Human
Rights Council, which remains open. It welcomed the Special Rapporteur on
counter-terrorism and human rights in 2018 and the Special Rapporteur on the right to
adequate housing in 2019. It has not been possible to accept some requests to visit owing to
the health situation linked to the coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic and later, in
2022, to the holding of national elections (for the presidency and the legislature).
B. Internal monitoring mechanisms3
7. Title VI of the French Constitution is entitled “International Treaties”. The fourteenth
paragraph of the preamble to the Constitution of 1946, which has constitutional force, states
that “the French Republic, faithful to its traditions, shall observe the rules of public
international law”. In line with these provisions, article 55 of the Constitution of 4 October
1958 “defines the conditions under which international treaties and agreements prevail over
statutory law”. The French Republic does not make meeting its international human rights
commitments conditional on their application by other States parties.
8. In accordance with the principles relating to the status of national institutions for the
promotion and protection of human rights (the Paris Principles),4 the Government is
supported by the National Consultative Commission on Human Rights, a national human
rights institution with category A status recognized by the United Nations. Through its
opinions and recommendations, the Commission – which is completely independent – offers
advice and proposals to authorities in the fields of human rights and humanitarian action, as
well as monitoring the French State’s fulfilment of its international commitments. The
authorities have entrusted the Commission with mandates as an independent national
rapporteur in many fields.5 It is systematically consulted on the State party reports submitted
to the treaty bodies.
9. The Economic, Social and Environmental Council advises the Government and helps
to formulate policy in its areas of competence. It can be consulted by the parliament and can
receive petitions from citizens. Its links with civil society were strengthened by an organic
law of 15 January 20216 (annex, sect. 1).
10. The Defender of Rights “shall ensure due respect for rights and freedoms by State
administrations, local authorities, public institutions and any bodies performing a public
service or by entities that, according to the organic law, fall within the Defender’s remit”
(Constitution, art. 71–1). The Defender is responsible for defending the rights of users of
public services; defending and promoting children’s rights; combating discrimination and
promoting equality; ensuring that security professionals comply with their codes of ethics;
and guiding and protecting whistle-blowers. Any citizen may refer a matter to the Defender
of Rights. It has the authority to investigate and to mediate and can offer plea agreements in
criminal cases. It can publish recommendations, either individual or general, and has the right
to follow up on them.
11. The High Council for Gender Equality is tasked with making recommendations,
contributing to the evaluation of public policy, monitoring legislative developments and
collecting and disseminating data,7 including through its annual report on the state of sexism
in France. It is consulted on bills and decrees intended to ensure gender equality in the
workplace. With its annual reports and its “sexism barometer”, it calls for action by the
authorities on all issues pertaining to gender equality.
GE.23-04198 3

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
III. Implementation of recommendations from the previous
cycles and of voluntary pledges
A. Voluntary pledges
12. As part of its candidacy for the Human Rights Council for the period from 2021 to
2023, France pledged to promote an ambitious feminist diplomacy agenda (see sect. III.E.),
to protect human rights defenders (see sect. III.B.6., Marianne Initiative), to defend the
freedom of the press and the right to reliable information (see sect. III.C.1) and to continue
its efforts to ensure the universalization and effectiveness of all civil and political, economic,
social and cultural rights (see sect. III.D.1, 2, 3 and 4).
B. Cross-cutting issues
1. COVID-19 pandemic
13. During the COVID-19 pandemic, in response to the public health emergency,8 France
imposed specific conditions to protect the population. Using this new mechanism, which was
subject to parliamentary oversight, the Government temporarily took, by means of decrees,
measures providing for periods of lockdown or curfew, restricting freedom of movement,
freedom of enterprise and freedom of assembly, and for the requisitioning of any goods and
services needed to respond to the health crisis, subject to judicial review.
14. A system of health passes – paper or digital (annex, sect. 2) – was implemented in
French territory from 9 June 2021 to accompany the easing of the public health measures and
the lifting of temporary curbs on freedoms. The authorities ensured the protection of citizens’
personal data in implementation of the health pass system.
15. A law that took effect on 1 August 2022 repealed the provisions of the Public Health
Code relating to the above-mentioned public health emergency.
16. The authorities did their utmost to ensure that students could continue to attend
schools and other educational establishments.
2. Climate change9
17. France supports a human rights-based approach to combating climate change, taking
into account the particular challenges faced by the populations most affected by this
phenomenon, in particular during the negotiation of international instruments on the
environment and climate.
18. France is implementing the Paris Agreement. It has published its revised National
Low Carbon Strategy (April 2020) and strengthened its climate governance, including by
establishing the High Council on Climate, an independent consultative body responsible for
assessing national climate policy. The fulfilment of its international commitments to combat
climate change is subject to full judicial review (annex, sect. 3).
19. The Act to Combat Climate Disruption and to Build Resilience to its Effects10
incorporates some of the proposals put forward in the Citizens’ Climate Convention of
January 2020.
3. Combating discrimination11
20. The equality of all citizens before the law, without distinction on grounds of origin,
race or religion, is a constitutional principle (Constitution, art. 1) (annex, sect. 4). Under that
principle, the granting of collective rights to any group defined by community of origin,
culture, language or belief is prohibited.
21. French law prohibits and punishes all forms of racism and antisemitism. Several
recent laws have stepped up the struggle against racism and discrimination, in particular the
Act to Combat Online Hate Content,12 which established an observatory of online hate, and
4 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
the Act on Respect for the Principles of the Republic,13 which provides a framework for the
measures taken by platforms to combat online hate.
22. Following a plan covering the period from 2018 to 2021, a new national plan to
combat racism, antisemitism and discrimination connected to origin14 was presented by the
Prime Minister on 30 January 2023, covering the period from 2023 to 2026 and incorporating
measures to combat racial discrimination, in particular with regard to gaining access to jobs,
in employment or in housing.
23. Drawn up in the context of a broad consultation process involving civil society, the
National Consultative Commission on Human Rights and the Defender of Rights, the new
plan has five core principles: (i) affirming the reality of racism, antisemitism and
discrimination and reaffirming our universalist model; (ii) measuring the phenomena of
racism, antisemitism and discrimination; (iii) educating and training better; (iv) punishing
perpetrators; and (v) supporting victims and guiding local policy. The plan comprises
80 concrete measures, accompanied by evaluation indicators. It will be monitored every six
months, with the involvement of stakeholders (annex, sect. 5).
24. Several large-scale surveys (annex, sect. 6) have helped to guide public policies to
counter discrimination, including racial discrimination (annex, sect. 7). In February 2021, the
Government launched the website www.antidiscriminations.fr, with the Defender of Rights
responsible for its development and management (annex, sect. 8). In accordance with article 1
of the Constitution (see above), France does not produce statistics disaggregated by racial or
ethnic origin. It does, however, support the development of tools for understanding
discrimination and, therefore, countering it more effectively (annex, sect. 9).
25. In view of the worrying growth of hate speech and hate crimes, French law
enforcement agencies are particularly active in combating those phenomena, including
online. Staffing levels at the PHAROS online platform for reporting illegal content and
behaviour, operated by the Ministry of the Interior and established in 2009, have been
doubled (annex, sect. 10). In August 2020, a hate crimes division was created within the
Central Office for Combating Crimes against Humanity and Hate Crimes (annex, sect. 11).
26. France has endorsed the non-legally binding working definition of antisemitism
adopted by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance and is implementing it in
practice (annex, sect. 12). Every year, France awards the Ilan Halimi Prize to groups of young
people in recognition of their efforts to counter stereotypes. In partnership with several
universities, the Mémorial de la Shoah and the French Union of Jewish Students,
“accountability measures” – for instance, participation in actions in support of solidarity or
cultural or educational activities – for students who commit racist and antisemitic acts have
been tried out; such measures were extended further in 2022. Agreements have been signed
between public prosecutor’s offices, specialized associations and places of remembrance on
organizing civics courses that can be used as an alternative to prosecution or imposed as an
additional penalty on perpetrators of racist or antisemitic offences.
27. Since 2015, the Month of Remembrance of Slavery and Struggles for Equality has
been celebrated in France. The Foundation for the Remembrance of Slavery coordinates the
project. With the Foundation’s recognition as a public service institution in 2019,15 the event
has become a concrete memorial commemorating the victims of slavery and those who
fought against it.
28. On 12 March 2021 the Council of the European Union, with the support of France,
issued a recommendation to ensure Roma equality, inclusion and participation,16 and a
corresponding French strategy (annex, sect. 13) for the period from 2020 to 2030 was sent to
the European Commission in February 2022. The measures included in section 1 of this
strategy (combating anti-Roma racism or anti-Gypsyism) have been incorporated into the
new national plan to combat racism, antisemitism and discrimination connected to origin,
presented on 30 January 2023.
29. For the protection and equal rights of LGBT+ persons, the French authorities adopted,
in October 2020, the national action plan for equal rights, against hatred and discrimination
towards LGBT+ persons, 2020–2023, which covers all areas of everyday life, including
health, education and sport (annex, sect. 14). A new offence and penalties have been added
GE.23-04198 5

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
to the Criminal Code17 in respect of conversion therapy. The Bioethics Act has made
medically assisted reproduction available to all-female couples.18 The Adoption Reform Act
has replaced the terms “father and mother” with “parents” and opened up adoption to
unmarried couples.19 A national communication campaign has been launched to counter
homophobia and transphobia.20 Measures have been taken to help LGBT+ refugees and
asylum-seekers, in particular in respect of the specifications for emergency accommodation
for asylum-seekers. On 26 October 2022, an Ambassador was appointed to speak and take
action on behalf of France to further the rights of LGBT+ persons, with the support of the
entire French diplomatic network.
30. The French authorities have stepped up their support for civil society associations
working to combat racism, antisemitism and hatred towards lesbian, gay, bisexual and
transgender persons. Funding for the interministerial delegation for combating racism,
antisemitism and hatred towards lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender persons doubled
between 2017 and 2023, enabling more than 1,000 organizations – associations, places of
remembrance, museums, public institutions and cultural centres – to benefit from State
support for prevention, education and anti-hatred schemes.
31. In order to foster equality of opportunity, regardless of social and geographic
background, France is taking concrete measures to reduce the unemployment rate in the
groups that face the greatest difficulties with social and labour-market integration, in
particular in priority urban development districts.
32. In the field of education, a major effort is being made to reduce the impact of social
and economic inequalities. In that connection, the For a School of Trust Act21 has lowered
the age of compulsory education from 6 to 3 years of age, made education compulsory up to
the age of 18 and reorganized teacher training to promote academic success. The Act, adopted
in 2019, in combination with the “Respect Others” programme, is intended to foster a culture
of kindness and tolerance between students, between students and teachers and between
adults. Academic focal points for gender equality and secularism have been appointed and
the number of school mediation officers has increased. The measures previously put in place
to promote success at school have been maintained; these include: the priority education
policy, under which increased resources can be channelled into students in 1,093 priority
education networks, located in the most disadvantaged areas of the country and in
neighbourhoods where social segregation is the most acute; the reduction to approximately
12 children per class in the first three years of primary school in these areas, a measure known
as “class-splitting” (annex, sect. 15); the “Homework Done” programme (annex, sect. 16);
and a progressive system of allocation of resources (annex, sect. 17).
4. Human rights and counter-terrorism22
33. Counter-terrorism measures taken by France respect the international framework for
human rights protection.
34. Since 1986, France has had extensive counter-terrorism laws, which have been
gradually strengthened in response to new forms of terrorism and new threats. In 2019, the
national counter-terrorism prosecution service 23 was established, with nationwide
jurisdiction, led by the national counter-terrorism prosecutor and incorporated into the Paris
judicial court.
35. The French judicial mechanism for counter-terrorism is based on specialized law, with
specific offences and procedural rules, and relies on specialized magistrates and investigation
agencies, with due respect for the adversarial principle, for the right to a fair, independent
and impartial trial and for due process.
36. The application of terrorism prevention and counter-terrorism provisions is subject to
judicial review. The Constitutional Council reviews the constitutionality of laws, in particular
their compliance with fundamental rights. It applies restrictions to measures deemed
unnecessary or disproportionate to the aim pursued and ensures that they are accompanied
by adequate safeguards and remedies (annex, sect. 18).
37. The Counter-terrorism and Intelligence Act24 provides for parliamentary oversight of
the counter-terrorism measures taken by administrative authorities with regard to protective
6 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
perimeters, to the closure of places of worship, to individual administrative monitoring and
surveillance measures and to searches and seizures.
5. Digital environment and human rights
38. Online and offline, France both applies international human rights law and advocates
for its full implementation, in particular the protection of privacy and personal data, freedom
of the press and freedom of expression. French law regulates the exercise of freedom of
expression both online and offline, in particular prohibiting abuse, defamation, racial hatred
and justification of terrorism.
39. The relevant laws have recently been supplemented in response to the challenges
posed by social networks:
• The Act to Combat Online Hate Content25 established an observatory of online hate.
The observatory provides an overview of the phenomenon and contributes to a better
understanding of it, which is essential for better targeting of legal action (annex,
sect. 19).
• The Act on Respect for the Principles of the Republic26 establishes a new offence of
endangering the life of another person by the dissemination of information relating to
a person’s private, family or professional life. It introduces a new regime for the
moderation of illegal online content by platforms, under the supervision of the
Regulatory Authority for Audiovisual and Digital Communication.
• A law enacted in 202027 governs the commercial exploitation of images of children
under 16 years of age on online platforms and the work of child influencers. It
establishes the right to be forgotten.
40. France is working at the international level to promote an open, free and safe Internet.
A priority of the French presidency of the Council of the European Union in 2022, the Digital
Services Act of 23 April 2022 ensures better democratic and economic regulation of
platforms and transparency in their content moderation activities, as well as combating illegal
and harmful content.
6. Development28
41. The defence of human rights is a priority of French development policy, enshrined in
the Act on Solidarity in Development and the Reduction of Global Inequalities,29 which
confirms the commitment of France to devoting 0.55 per cent of its gross national income to
official development assistance by 2022 and its target of reaching 0.70 per cent by 2025.
42. In the period from 2020 to 2024, France is implementing the first action plan under
its new “Human Rights and Development” strategy, in which a human rights-based approach
to development cooperation is adopted. France has undertaken a number of initiatives in this
area:
• The Marianne Initiative, launched in December 2021 by the President of the Republic,
protects and supports human rights defenders in their own countries and through a
programme of hosting and support in France (annex, sect. 20).
• On 19 June 2021, a road map was adopted for international action by France in relation
to civil status, in order to promote access to rights and the economic and social
development of partner States.
• The Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration was signed in 2018 and
the Global Compact on Refugees was signed in 2019. France launched the
“International Migration and Development” action plan, 2018–2022.
• France set the target of ensuring that 50 per cent of its official development assistance
contributes, directly or indirectly, to gender equality by 2022. This ambitious target
was increased to 75 per cent by the Act of 4 August 202130 (annex, sect. 21) (see
sect. E., on gender equality).
43. France has made children’s rights one of its foreign policy priorities.31 France has
acceded to all international instruments protecting and promoting children’s rights (annex,
sect. 22).
GE.23-04198 7

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
44. In June 2019, France took over the Chair of the Global Alliance to Eradicate Forced
Labour, Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Child Labour (Alliance 8.7), which brings
together States, international organizations, social partners, businesses and
non-governmental organizations. In 2021, the Government adopted a National Acceleration
Strategy to Eliminate Child Labour, Forced Labour, Human Trafficking and Contemporary
Slavery by 2030, developed in collaboration with French stakeholders.32
45. The French authorities are fully committed to ensuring inclusive and equitable access
to quality education for all children, in particular girls and the most vulnerable and
marginalized children, including in emergency and crisis situations. This takes the form of
bilateral projects to strengthen education systems and capacities in developing countries and
of participation in regional or global funds, such as the Global Partnership for Education, to
which France is the third-largest donor (annex, sect. 23).
C. Civil and political rights
1. Protecting journalists and freedom of the press and countering disinformation33
46. France is committed to respecting freedom of expression and opinion, from which
freedom of the press stems. These are not absolute, however, and are subject to restrictions
laid down by international and statute law, such as respect for the individual, the protection
of minors and the prevention of abuse, defamation and invasion of privacy. Misuse of
freedom of expression may be sanctioned only by a judge. France is adapting its legislative
framework to combating illegal online content, while fully respecting freedom of expression,
by imposing stricter obligations on the chief online platforms to prevent illegal hate content,34
counter the manipulation of information 35 and ensure transparency in their
content-moderation activities (annex, sect. 24).
47. The Act to Combat Information Manipulation 36 is intended to prevent the
manipulation of information, in particular during election periods (annex, sect. 25). It
establishes a duty of cooperation for digital platforms and search engines (annex, sect. 26)
and obligates them to combat the dissemination of false information likely to disrupt public
order or undermine the reliability of elections.
48. France has strengthened its legislative framework for protecting journalists,
safeguarding their sources and defending free, independent and pluralistic media.37 The status
of “whistle-blower” has been established in French law, which is among the most protective
in the world.38
49. In 2019, in partnership with Reporters Without Borders and with the support of
50 States from all regions of the world, France launched the International Partnership for
Information and Democracy with a view to promoting the exercise of freedom of expression
and of the press, to fostering access to reliable information and to guaranteeing the economic
sustainability of the media (annex, sect. 27). It takes in journalists who are under threat and
provides €43 million in support for independent media projects in various countries.
2. Respect for human rights by the security forces39
50. The actions of the police and gendarmerie are governed by law and are subject to
monitoring by the Defender of Rights and by the police and gendarmerie inspectorates.
51. France is careful not to allow the disproportionate use of force to go unpunished and
guarantees persons who consider themselves victims of such acts the right to an effective
remedy (annex, sect. 28). Officers suspected of excessive use of force may be subject to
administrative and judicial investigations. The use of force is monitored by the administrative
hierarchy and the national police and gendarmerie inspectorates, with inappropriate recourse
to it being punished (annex, sect. 29). When a breach is identified, police officers and
gendarmes are subject to disciplinary sanctions (annex, sect. 30). In the event of legal
proceedings, in order to guarantee the principle of impartiality, public prosecutor’s offices
regularly transfer the case to a court in another part of the country when the gendarmerie or
police officer in question is in frequent contact with the judges of the court initially competent
to try the case (annex, sect. 31).
8 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
52. France condemns all ethnic profiling measures, which violate the constitutional
principle of equality. The domestic security forces’ prerogatives with regard to identity
checks are strictly regulated (annex, sect. 32); in particular, racial profiling is prohibited
(annex, sect. 33). The public prosecutor ensures that all checks are conducted in accordance
with all relevant laws and regulations.40 Any officers conducting checks that violate the rules
will be subject to disciplinary sanctions imposed by their senior officers41 and to action by
the judicial authorities in their capacity as guardians of individual freedoms. The struggle
against racial profiling has been stepped up: persons considering themselves to be victims of
such acts have the right to an effective remedy and may claim compensation for the
non-pecuniary damage they have suffered due to gross negligence on the part of the State.42
It is also possible to report such violations online, to the police or gendarmerie inspectorate
or to the Defender of Rights.
53. The French authorities ensure that the prohibition of torture is respected. Under
French law, subjecting a person to torture or barbaric acts is an offence punishable by
15 years’ imprisonment, increased to 20 years when committed by persons acting in an
official capacity or while providing a public service in the performance of or during their
functions or work (annex, sect. 34).
54. A code of ethics common to both the police force and the gendarmerie is issued to all
police officers and gendarmes when they enter service (annex, sect. 35). Police officers and
gendarmes receive initial and in-service training to enable them to discharge their duties in
an irreproachable manner. The common rules governing the use of weapons by law
enforcement officers, introduced into the Internal Security Code by the Public Security Act,43
clarify the rules governing the use of arms and incorporate the principles laid down by the
European Court of Human Rights and domestic courts (annex, sect. 36).
55. In accordance with the requirements of the Court of Justice of the European Union,44
communications surveillance and other intelligence-gathering techniques are strictly
regulated. France has enhanced the oversight of the National Commission for the Control of
Intelligence Techniques, an independent administrative authority.45 In the judicial context,
the Act on Planning for 2018–2022 and Judicial Reform46 provides that only a court may, for
a limited period of time, authorize the interception of communications, when it deems it
necessary for ascertaining the truth, and when such action is commensurate with the gravity
of the offence (annex 37). The judicial use of special investigative techniques is subject to
greater scrutiny by the courts. In addition, the Counter-terrorism and Intelligence Act47
specified that operators could be required to retain connection data, but only in the event of
a serious threat, for national security purposes and for a period of one year, with a view to
preventing serious offences and punishing their perpetrators. The School Bullying Prevention
Act 48 strengthens the framework for requesting connection data during criminal
investigations.
3. Places of detention49
56. Major reforms have been undertaken to improve conditions of detention.
57. In addition to the existing emergency procedures brought before administrative judges
for interim relief, a law passed in 2021 enables any persons held in prison to challenge before
the ordinary courts conditions of detention that they consider to be contrary to human
dignity50 (annex, sect. 38).
58. The French authorities work to prevent suicide by developing staff training, detection
procedures and protection measures (annex, sect. 39).
59. The voting rights of prisoners have been strengthened. They can now vote by post.51
60. The legislation was changed in 2019 and 2021 to encourage the use of rehabilitation
and of alternative measures to incarceration (annex, sect. 40).52
61. The French authorities are carrying out a programme to expand and renovate the
country’s prisons as part of their policy to combat overcrowding. In this respect, the 2019
law provides for the creation of 15,000 additional places (annex, sect. 41), new forms of
detention (annex, sect. 42) and the closure or renovation of dilapidated facilities.
GE.23-04198 9

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
62. The law guarantees free communication between prisoners and defence counsel,
prohibits the monitoring of telephone conversations between prisoners and their lawyers and
establishes the principle of accommodation in individual cells (annex, sect. 43). It emphasizes
that access to care must be continuous and of high quality and that the psychological state of
prisoners must be taken into account.
63. For minors, there are three types of facilities (annex, sect. 44): juvenile wards,
specialized prisons and seven new units for underage girls, supported by specially trained
teams (annex, sect. 45). The Charter of Rights and Freedoms at Institutions, which is
applicable to closed educational centres, includes instructions to ensure respect for the right
to family contacts, freedom of worship and access to information.53
64. Persons deprived of their liberty in the context of French overseas operations benefit
from procedural and material guarantees, in accordance with international law.
Administrative detention by the military must be justified by compelling reasons of national
security (annex, sect. 46).
4. Freedom of religion or belief
65. France defends freedom of religion and belief. It is enshrined in the major
international instruments it has ratified and has constitutional status.54 The principle of
secularism allows the exercise of all beliefs, subject only to respect for public order and the
fundamental freedoms of each individual. The neutrality of the State and public authorities,
an essential condition for equality between citizens, is a fundamental requirement (annex,
sect. 47).
66. All rights relating to freedom of religion are thus guaranteed for every individual,
subject to limited exceptions provided for by law, motivated by considerations of public order
and subject to review by the courts. The 2004 law banning conspicuous religious symbols in
public schools is designed to protect underage children from possible pressure or
discrimination. The 2010 law prohibiting people from concealing their faces in public55 takes
into account the principle of equality and respect for the dignity of each individual, while
safeguarding the minimum requirements for living in society.
67. The 2021 law56 reaffirms freedom of worship while giving the State the means to
combat radicalism. This reform strengthened the requirements for transparency and good
governance of religious associations and granted them new benefits.
5. Combating trafficking in persons57
68. Combating human trafficking is a priority in the fight against organized crime.
69. After adopting a first national action plan to combat trafficking in 2014, France is now
implementing a second plan (for 2019–2022) (annex, sect. 48). A third action plan is
currently being drawn up. An Interministerial Task Force to Protect Women against Violence
and to Combat Human Trafficking was established in 2013 with the aim of strengthening the
protection of victims of trafficking (annex, sect. 49). Funding for the fight against trafficking
is to be increased by €1 million in 2023, using proceeds from confiscations through the
Agency for the management and recovery of seized criminal assets.
70. With regard to the victims of prostitution, the Act of 13 April 2016 aimed at
strengthening the fight against prostitution and supporting prostitutes established the right for
victims of prostitution to benefit from a system of protection and assistance. It established
the offence of purchasing a sexual act provided by a person of the age of majority. A national
monitoring committee ensures its proper implementation.
71. In November 2021, the national plan to combat underage prostitution called for a
financial commitment of €14 million (annex, sect. 50). The aim is to better identify child
victims and support them throughout their lives while cracking down harder on customers
and pimps. The plan calls for an alignment of penalties for human trafficking and pimping of
minors, the appointment of a reference official to deal with juvenile prostitution in each
public prosecutor’s office and the development of cyberinvestigations.
10 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
72. At the international level, France is actively working towards the universalization and
effective implementation of the only universal binding legal instrument to combat human
trafficking: the Protocol to Prevent, Suppress and Punish Trafficking in Persons, Especially
Women and Children, supplementing the United Nations Convention against Transnational
Organized Crime. France is developing close cooperation in the regions most affected by this
phenomenon.
D. Economic, social and cultural rights
1. Combating poverty and exclusion; access to housing58
73. In 2018, France adopted a national strategy to prevent and combat poverty. It is based
on five commitments: equal opportunities from the earliest age; guarantee of a training path
for all young people; guarantee in everyday life of the fundamental rights of children; more
accessible and equitable social rights that incentivize work; and assistance for all in finding
employment. The latest progress report on the strategy (annex, sect. 51) shows the advances
made in meeting these commitments.
74. The five-year Housing First Plan, launched in 2017, constitutes the housing
component of this strategy and provides funding to strengthen housing retention and access
to emergency accommodation. In the fight against homelessness, the plan has already
produced concrete results: more than 330,000 homeless people have gained access to housing
since 2017.
75. During the COVID-19 pandemic, exceptional resources were mobilized. Some
43,000 accommodation places were opened beginning in March 2020, bringing emergency
accommodation capacity to a record level of 200,000 places, and were kept open until March
2022, and 1,000 emergency accommodation places for women victims of domestic violence
were created in 2021–2022 to meet the needs identified by associations during the first
lockdown.
76. France has stepped up its efforts to integrate people from illegal settlements and
shantytowns. The respective national budget allocation has more than doubled (€8 million in
2020) (annex, sect. 52).
2. Human rights and business59
77. With the 2017 law on the duty of care of parent companies and companies placing
orders,60 France was among the first States to adopt an ambitious legislative framework
relating to the human rights obligations of businesses. Initial assessments of the law’s
implementation show that it has indeed raised awareness of corporate responsibility among
companies, despite the fact that they have committed to it and taken it on board to varying
degrees61 (annex, sect. 53).
78. At European level, France is taking part in the discussions under way in the Council
of the European Union on the draft directive on the duty of care62 (annex, sect. 54) presented
by the European Commission on 26 February 2022.
79. At the international level, France takes part in the work of the open-ended
intergovernmental working group on transnational corporations and other business
enterprises with respect to human rights63
3. Right to health64
80. In France, the right to health is a constitutional principle: paragraph 11 of the preamble
to the 1946 Constitution, which was also taken up in the 1958 Constitution, states that “the
Nation guarantees health protection for all”.
81. Since 2016, universal health protection has guaranteed anyone who works or resides
in France on a stable and regular basis (annex, sect. 55), and also their minor beneficiaries,
entitlement to coverage of their health-care costs on a personal basis and throughout their
life.
GE.23-04198 11

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
82. The State Medical Assistance scheme, introduced in 1999,65 is designed to provide
health coverage for destitute foreigners in an irregular situation based on a three-fold
rationale: protecting public health, respecting the right to health and controlling public
expenditure. This assistance is available to all foreigners in an irregular situation, provided
that their household income does not exceed 767 euros per month for one person66 and that
they have been residing in France uninterruptedly and in an irregular situation for more than
three months. Asylum-seekers are immediately entitled to emergency care.
83. The National Health Strategy (2018–2022) pays particular attention to social
inequalities in health. France has thus introduced new schemes such as mobile health-care
beds (annex, sect. 56) and health-care mobile teams and home nursing services to serve
people living in precarious conditions; these consolidate the services provided to people
living in vulnerable or precarious situations.
84. Faced with the COVID-19 pandemic, France deployed a road map for mental health
and psychiatry, with three main themes: preventing mental suffering and combating
stigmatization of persons with mental disabilities; building up individualized, high-quality
and coordinated care with priority given to outpatient care; and ensuring social inclusion and
citizenship for persons with mental disabilities. France provides psychological support for
persons who have been traumatized by persecution or exile (annex, sect. 57).
4. Access to culture
85. France has stepped up its cultural democratization efforts, by introducing a Culture
Pass for young people. By awarding a €300 grant to 18-year-olds (annex, sect. 58), this
scheme makes it possible for young people to independently make their cultural choices.
E. Gender equality
1. Promoting gender equality67
86. Equality between women and men has been declared a national priority by the
President of France.
87. A law passed in 2021 to strengthen equality in the economy and in the world of work68
introduced a gender parity quota for senior managers and board members of companies with
1,000 employees or more, and a corresponding requirement for disclosure (annex, sect. 59).
The French Sports Code69 has been amended to introduce a strict parity requirement for all
sports federation governing bodies (annex, sect. 60).
88. In the field of equal pay, France has introduced an obligation for all companies with
at least 50 employees to be transparent and demonstrate their results, with the creation of the
Gender Equality in Employment Index.70 This system, which gives companies an overall
score out of 100 points based on four or five indicators, depending on their size, is designed
to objectively measure gender gaps in remuneration and status while identifying areas for
corrective action (annex, sect. 61).
89. France has taken various measures to ensure that its civil service, the country’s largest
employer, sets an example in terms of gender equality in the workplace. As a reminder,
63 per cent of civil servants are women. In 2020, each government minister submitted his or
her action plan for equality in the workplace (annex, sect. 62). Women accounted for
43 per cent of new appointments to all senior and executive positions in the civil service,
compared with 42 per cent in 2019. Within the national police force, the role of women is
highlighted by the Ariane programme, which aims to identify and support women, with the
use of specific tools (annex, sect. 63).
90. To encourage fathers’ involvement in childcare, France has increased the duration of
paternity leave paid by social security71 to 25 days (compared with 11 previously), plus three
days of birth leave, paid by the employer.
91. France has continued its efforts to protect victims against all forms of violence,
including sexual harassment (see sect. E.2.).
12 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
92. France has made the promotion of women’s and girls’ rights and the fight against
gender-based discrimination a cross-cutting objective of its foreign action, in line with its
International Strategy for Gender Equality (2018–2022). Its feminist diplomacy, formalized
in 2019, is carried out both bilaterally and in international forums. Under the aegis of the
United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN-Women),
in June 2021, France hosted the Generation Equality Forum in Paris, and co-chaired it with
Mexico. At the Forum, a global plan to accelerate progress for gender equality was launched.
France pledged €400 million to support sexual and reproductive health and rights and
women’s health. The French presidency at the G7 summit held in Biarritz in 2019 led to the
launch of initiatives in the period 2020–2022: the Biarritz Partnership for Gender Equality,
an alliance of countries committed to adopting advanced legislation on women’s rights; the
African Development Bank’s Affirmative Financial Action for Women in Africa (AFAWA)
programme, designed to give African women entrepreneurs access to financing; and the
Global Survivors Fund for and with Survivors of Conflict-related Sexual Violence,
co-founded by Nobel Peace Prize laureates Nadia Murad and Denis Mukwege.
93. France has actively contributed to the adoption and implementation of the
United Nations Security Council’s women and peace and security agenda resolutions. It
adopted the third national action plan on the implementation of the women and peace and
security resolutions (2021–2025) for greater participation by women in peacekeeping
operations and peacebuilding and for better protection from violence in times of conflict.
94. A new four-year equality plan is currently being finalized and will be released at the
meeting of the Interministerial Committee for Gender Equality, to be held on 8 March 2023
under the auspices of the Prime Minister. This four-year plan will focus on four areas for the
period 2023–2027: (i) combating violence against women; (ii) economic equality; (iii) a
culture of equality; and (iv) women’s health.
2. Combating sexual and gender-based violence72
95. France has continued its efforts to protect victims against all forms of violence,
including sexual harassment.
96. France is mobilized in the fight against domestic and intimate partner violence, both
physical and sexual, which is one of the most common forms of violence against women. In
2019, France held the first national round table against domestic violence, which gave rise to
102 local events involving over 4,550 people and set up 51 local victim support committees.
A national strategy to combat domestic violence has been put in place to: (i) better prevent
violence; (ii) provide greater protection for victims and their children; and (iii) set up a
follow-up and assistance system for perpetrators of violence to prevent them from
reoffending.
97. The commitments undertaken during the national round table have resulted in
46 concrete measures (annex, sect. 64), including 10 emergency measures, such as:
• Extending the possibility of placing convicted domestic violence offenders under
mobile electronic surveillance73
• Facilitating the eviction of domestic violence perpetrators from the marital home as
an alternative to prosecution74
• Extending the use of anti-approach bracelets at all stages of criminal proceedings to
prevent any encounters within a safety zone defined by the court75
• Making it possible for health-care workers to report domestic violence without the
victim’s consent76
98. These initial measures were reinforced with the announcement of six new measures
in November 2021, including two groundbreaking steps:
• The distribution of 5,000 danger alert phones (that enable victims to alert the police
in the event of a serious danger)
• The establishment of a specific follow-up file for perpetrators of domestic violence
• A tightening of controls on the possession and acquisition of weapons
GE.23-04198 13

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
99. Consistent with these measures, on 8 March 2023 an Interministerial Committee for
Gender Equality will be launched, along with a new plan, the first theme of which is dedicated
to combating violence against women.
100. A plan to eradicate female genital mutilation was also presented in 2019 (annex,
sect. 65).
101. France is strengthening its legal framework against sexual harassment and sexist
behaviour in the workplace.77 The definition of sexual harassment in the Labour Code, in line
with the one in the Criminal Code, offers a high level of protection. This definition was
clarified by a law in 2021, according to which sexist comments and behaviour constitute
sexual harassment.78 An offence was also established under the title of sexist or sexual
offence of a person’s dignity. French case law covers both sexual harassment in the
workplace and outside the workplace or working hours, when there is an abuse of hierarchical
power for the purpose of obtaining sexual favours. However, the number of criminal
convictions has remained low. New measures to facilitate the prosecution and punishment of
sexual violence will be announced on 8 March 2023.
102. During its presidency of the Council of the European Union, France convened a
ministerial conference on 7 March 2022 on the mobilization of European law enforcement
agencies against domestic violence. Prior to this, on a French initiative, member and associate
States drew up a handbook of best practices for European law enforcement agencies in
preventing violence against women and in receiving, caring for and protecting victims of
domestic violence (annex, sect. 66). France initiated the Council’s work on the draft new
European directive on combating violence against women and domestic violence.
103. At the international level, France promotes the Council of Europe Convention on
Preventing and Combating Violence against Women and Domestic Violence, known as the
Istanbul Convention. France, which has held the Presidency of the Committee of the Parties
to the Convention since 1 February 2022, launched campaigns to promote this instrument
and make it universal with the accession of States outside the Council of Europe.
104. In the fight against impunity, France advocates for including a designation for
perpetrators of sexual violence, within the framework of United Nations sanctions regimes
(annex, sect. 67).
F. Rights of persons belonging to specific groups
1. Children
Child protection79
105. France carries out a policy that promotes the full development of the child, with
protection for the child, education and youth and support for parenthood and family policy.
The main thrusts of this policy have been set out in the Pact for Childhood (annex, sect. 68),
launched in 2019.
106. In 2020, France launched the second interministerial plan to mobilize and combat
violence against children. It is aimed at raising public awareness and proposing concrete
solutions to curb all forms of violence within the family.
107. In France, violence of any kind, including psychological violence, is punishable under
the Criminal Code. Penalties are increased when the violence in question is committed
against a minor under the age of 15 (annex, sect. 69). Neglect is punishable, in particular
when minors are left unprotected and unable to protect themselves, or when parents deprive
minors of care and nourishment or jeopardize their health, safety, morals or education.
108. Henceforth, holders of parental authority must exercise their authority without
physical, verbal or psychological violence (including spanking), castigation or humiliation
of the child.80
109. France has amended its legislation on child consent, introducing a presumption of
non-consent to better protect minors against sexual crimes and incest81 (annex, sect. 70).
14 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
110. It has strengthened the contribution of the child welfare services82 to protection against
violence through various measures: systematic checking of the criminal records of
professionals and volunteers; establishment of anti-mistreatment policies at each
establishment; and reporting, with the use of a single, shared reference system.
Protecting the rights of minors in the context of immigration and asylum83
111. France has a system for the protection of unaccompanied minors present on its
territory regardless of their nationality or legal situation. Under the Child Protection Act,84
unaccompanied minors are placed throughout the country in order to guarantee that they have
the same rights as any other child in France. This law strictly regulates the use of age
assessment using bone tests, specifies that the person being assessed must be given the benefit
of the doubt and prohibits certain medical examinations that may be detrimental to personal
dignity.
112. Child protection has been strengthened by a law for controlled immigration, an
effective right to asylum and successful integration85 (known as the Asylum and Immigration
Act). Henceforth, when asylum applications are lodged by foreign nationals in France with
their minor children, the application is deemed to be lodged on their behalf and also on behalf
of their children (annex, sect. 71).
Juvenile criminal justice86
113. By adopting a Juvenile Criminal Justice Code, which entered into force on
30 September 2021, France has reaffirmed the main principles of its juvenile criminal justice
system:87 a reduction of criminal responsibility of minors according to their age, or
extenuating circumstances on grounds of their youth; the priority given to education over
repression; and the use of specialized courts and procedures.
114. France has introduced the presumption of non-discernment for minors under the age
of 13, in accordance with article 40 of the Convention on the Rights of the Child (annex,
sect. 72). Minors aged between 13 and 18 are presumed to have acted with discernment until
proven otherwise.
115. The means of providing educational assistance have been strengthened. France has
established a unique educational measure that can be modulated and adapted over time,
taking into account the minor’s individual characteristics and development. Judicial
educational measures can thus be composed of modules (addressing integration,
compensation, health, placement or a prohibition against appearing in places or coming into
contact with victims or co-perpetrators). The conditions for using security measures such as
pretrial detention have been made more restrictive.
116. The principle of the use of specialized courts and procedures, provided for in juvenile
criminal justice, has been extended. In addition to the specialized courts and chambers,
certain liberties and detention judges are now specifically responsible for juvenile cases. The
new Code ensures continuous involvement throughout the proceedings by the minor’s
lawyer. The specialized procedures ensure that minors are informed of their rights in simple,
accessible terms.
2. Persons with disabilities88
117. France ensures that disability is taken into account in all its public policies and that
the provisions of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities are respected.
118. In particular, France actively supports the employment and career development of
persons with disabilities, in particular in the civil service. The obligations of public sector
employers towards persons with disabilities have been strengthened. New rights have been
created for such public sector employees, including the right to consult a disability adviser
and to benefit from portable workstation adaptation equipment if they are called upon to work
with mobility.89
119. France promotes the mobility of persons with disabilities through various measures,
including the widespread application for companions of persons with disabilities of
preferential transportation fares, up to and including free travel, and the strengthening of
GE.23-04198 15

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
access to transport services for persons with reduced mobility. Accessibility ambassadors
were deployed throughout the country in 2022, including 1,000 young people recruited under
the civil service programme.
120. Since its last report, France has instituted a public service of inclusive schools so that
families can send their children to school from the age of 3, be better heard and understood
and be able to meet support workers and educational teams at the start of the new school
year.90 In 2021, more than 400,000 pupils with disabilities were able to attend school,
supported by 125,500 support workers.
121. With the aim of making the digital world more accessible to persons with disabilities,
France has reaffirmed that the authorities are obliged to make their websites and mobile sites
accessible, introducing new obligations for administrative services91 (annex, sect. 73).
122. The following advances have been made in the area of legal protection for adults:92
• Removal of the requirement for a court’s prior authorization for protected persons to
decide to marry, enter into a civil partnership or divorce
• For personal consent to receive care, the court may intervene only in cases of
disagreement between protected adults and the persons in charge of their protection,
including for “serious acts”.
• Repeal of article L.5 of the Electoral Code, which gave the court the power to
withdraw the right to vote in a guardianship decision, in favour of a framework for
conditions for proxy voting
• “Uncoupling” of the adult disability allowance from 1 October 2023, ending the
practice of taking into account the income of the spouses of adults living with
disabilities in the calculation of their allowances
• Raising that allowance by 4 per cent, retroactive to 1 July 2022
• This subject should receive particular attention in the new equality plan currently
being prepared, which includes specific measures to prevent violence against women
in vulnerable situations
3. Foreigners, refugees and asylum-seekers93
123. France guarantees access to justice and rights for migrants, particularly those who
have been placed or kept in waiting zones or who have been denied residence permits.
124. It guarantees access to health care for all destitute foreign nationals residing illegally
in the country, through the State Medical Assistance scheme.94
125. The Asylum and Immigration Act95 has improved the processing of applications and
conditions for reception of applicants. The material care of asylum-seekers has been
strengthened, as asylum-seekers are given guidance, and follow-up of persons in
accommodation has been improved. Asylum-seekers are accommodated in reception centres
for asylum-seekers or similar structures (annex, sect. 74). France respects the right to
residency of people in need of protection, in particular by removing the residency
requirement for the family members of refugees applying for residency permits.
126. Access to the job market is supported by a wide range of professional support schemes
for new arrivals, including beneficiaries of international protection. A national programme
for integration helps new arrivals to become self-sufficient and to make use of the tools made
available under ordinary law (annex, sect. 75). A comprehensive individualized support
programme for refugees was established in 2022 to systematize support for migrants in
finding employment and housing and to ensure synergy with measures provided under the
ordinary law.
127. In the Calais and Grande-Synthe areas of the Hauts-de-France region, the treatment
of migrants is closely monitored by government services. In terms of access to drinking water
and hygiene, assistance has been in place since 2017 (annex, sect. 76).
128. In Mayotte, the asylum procedure has been adapted,96 in particular by reducing the
time taken to process applications.
16 GE.23-04198

A/HRC/WG.6/43/FRA/1
129. Faced with the consequences of the Russian war of aggression against Ukraine, France
adopted an instruction on 10 March 2022 listing the categories of people entitled to temporary
protection and the rights associated with this entitlement.
G. Overseas departments, regions and communities (or overseas
territories)97
130. Since its last report, France has implemented the commitments made under the 2017
Act on Genuine Equality Overseas98 to enable the territories to freely choose, together with
the State, their model of social, economic and cultural development, making the most of their
assets, identity and specific features.
131. The National Health Strategy 2018–2022 and the overseas component of the National
Sexual Health Strategy – Agenda 2017–2030 address the specific health issues in overseas
France and aim to reduce the significant gaps that exist between the overseas territories and
mainland France.
132. The new 2019–2022 overseas housing plan includes a section on combating
substandard housing. It aims in particular to improve knowledge of needs and to adapt the
housing supply.
133. Access to high-quality drinking water in overseas France, meeting health standards at
a reasonable cost, is a priority targeted by the DOM Water Plan launched in 2016.
134. As in mainland France, the protection and promotion of women’s rights is a priority.
Each overseas prefecture has a women’s rights delegate (annex, sect. 77).
135. In order to compensate possible victims of nuclear testing in French Polynesia, France
has authorized the reconsideration of claims that have already been denied.99 On 28 July
2021, in Papeete, the President announced several additional measures, including a three-year
extension of the deadline for filing compensation claims for nuclear test victims and their
heirs.
136. In keeping with the principle of the indivisibility of the Republic, France is pursuing
ambitious policies in favour of indigenous people, as illustrated by the creation of the
Consultative Council of Amerindian and Bushinenge Peoples.100
IV. International humanitarian law
137. Action by France in operations in other countries is carried out in compliance with
international humanitarian law. France promotes respect for humanitarian law in
international forums and at the United Nations Security Council. In 2019, France launched
the Call for Humanitarian Action with Germany as part of the Alliance for Multilateralism.
It is aimed at mobilizing the international community to better implement international
humanitarian law and preserve humanitarian space.
138. As part of its Presidency of the Council of the European Union, France and the
European Commission co-organized the first European Humanitarian Forum in Brussels in
March 2022, bringing together member States, countries that signed on to the Call for
Humanitarian Action and key humanitarian actors.
